annotator_overrides.question:
In the double-marginalization set-up the ratio of the retailer's margin over the manufacturer's margin,
\[
\frac{p - p_w}{p_w - c},
\]
is equal to (greater than, lower than) which number if the demand function is linear (convex, concave).
annotator_overrides.solution:
Let
\[
m_R \equiv p-p_w
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
m_M \equiv p_w-c
\]
denote the retailer's margin and the manufacturer's margin, respectively. We want to compute
\[
\frac{m_R}{m_M}=\frac{p-p_w}{p_w-c}.
\]

The key result is:
\[
\frac{p-p_w}{p_w-c}
=
\frac{1}{\,2-\dfrac{D(p)D''(p)}{[D'(p)]^2}\,}.
\]
Therefore,

\[
\frac{p-p_w}{p_w-c}
=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{1}{2}, & \text{if } D''(p)=0 \text{ (linear demand)},\\[1em]
>\dfrac{1}{2}, & \text{if } D''(p)>0 \text{ (convex demand)},\\[1em]
<\dfrac{1}{2}, & \text{if } D''(p)<0 \text{ (concave demand)}.
\end{cases}
\]

We now derive this result step by step.

The retailer solves
\[
\max_p (p-p_w)D(p).
\]
The first-order condition is
\begin{equation}
D(p)+(p-p_w)D'(p)=0.
\tag{1}
\end{equation}
Rearranging,
\begin{equation}
p-p_w=-\frac{D(p)}{D'(p)}.
\tag{2}
\end{equation}

Equation (1) implicitly defines the retailer's optimal price \(p^*(p_w)\). Differentiating (1) with respect to \(p_w\), we get
\[
D'(p)\frac{dp^*}{dp_w}
+
\left(\frac{dp^*}{dp_w}-1\right)D'(p)
+
(p-p_w)D''(p)\frac{dp^*}{dp_w}
=0.
\]
Collecting terms,
\[
\left[2D'(p)+(p-p_w)D''(p)\right]\frac{dp^*}{dp_w}-D'(p)=0.
\]
Hence,
\[
\frac{dp^*}{dp_w}
=
\frac{D'(p)}{\,2D'(p)+(p-p_w)D''(p)\,}.
\]
Using \((2)\), namely \(p-p_w=-D(p)/D'(p)\), this becomes
\begin{equation}
\frac{dp^*}{dp_w}
=
\frac{1}{\,2-\dfrac{D(p)D''(p)}{[D'(p)]^2}\,}.
\tag{3}
\end{equation}

Next, the manufacturer chooses \(p_w\) to maximize
\[
(p_w-c)D\bigl(p^*(p_w)\bigr).
\]
Its first-order condition is
\begin{equation}
D(p)+(p_w-c)D'(p)\frac{dp^*}{dp_w}=0.
\tag{4}
\end{equation}
Rearranging,
\begin{equation}
p_w-c
=
-\frac{D(p)}{D'(p)\,\dfrac{dp^*}{dp_w}}.
\tag{5}
\end{equation}

Now divide \((2)\) by \((5)\):
\[
\frac{p-p_w}{p_w-c}
=
\frac{-\dfrac{D(p)}{D'(p)}}{-\dfrac{D(p)}{D'(p)\,\dfrac{dp^*}{dp_w}}}
=
\frac{dp^*}{dp_w}.
\]
Using \((3)\), we obtain
\begin{equation}
\frac{p-p_w}{p_w-c}
=
\frac{1}{\,2-\dfrac{D(p)D''(p)}{[D'(p)]^2}\,}.
\tag{6}
\end{equation}

Therefore:

\begin{itemize}
\item If demand is linear, then \(D''(p)=0\), so
\[
\frac{p-p_w}{p_w-c}=\frac{1}{2}.
\]

\item If demand is convex, then \(D''(p)>0\). Since \(D(p)>0\) and \([D'(p)]^2>0\), we have
\[
\frac{D(p)D''(p)}{[D'(p)]^2}>0,
\]
so the denominator in \((6)\) is smaller than \(2\). Hence,
\[
\frac{p-p_w}{p_w-c}>\frac{1}{2}.
\]

\item If demand is concave, then \(D''(p)<0\), so the denominator in \((6)\) is larger than \(2\). Hence,
\[
\frac{p-p_w}{p_w-c}<\frac{1}{2}.
\]
\end{itemize}

In conclusion,
\[
\boxed{
\frac{p-p_w}{p_w-c}
=
\frac{1}{2}\ \text{for linear demand,}
\quad
>\frac{1}{2}\ \text{for convex demand,}
\quad
<\frac{1}{2}\ \text{for concave demand.}
}
\]

before_annotation.answer: (i) To obtain the equivalence, let firm 1 be the manufacturer and let firm 2 be the retailer. $p_1$ stands for the wholesale price $p_w$, $p_2 \equiv p - p_w$; $p$ is the price of the final good; $c_1$ is the unit manufacturing cost; $c_2$ is the unit retail cost (zero in the text).
before_annotation.question:
Two firms ($i=1, 2$) produce one good each, at marginal cost $c_i$ $(i = 1, 2)$. Each firm has a monopoly power in the production of its good. The goods are perfect complements. The demand curve is $q = D(p)$, where $p \equiv p_1 + p_2$ is the price of the composite good and $p_i$ is the price of good $i$ $(i = 1, 2)$. Let $c \equiv  c_1 + c_2$.

(i) Reinterpret the variables to show that the case of a single good produced by a manufacturer and distributed by a retailer fits into this model.

Assume that the elasticity of demand, $\varepsilon = -D'p/D$, is constant in order to simplify computations.

reference_answer_latex_from_by_problem:
(i) To obtain the equivalence, let firm 1 be the manufacturer and let firm 2 be the retailer. $p_1$ stands for the wholesale price $p_w$, $p_2 \equiv p - p_w$; $p$ is the price of the final good; $c_1$ is the unit manufacturing cost; $c_2$ is the unit retail cost (zero in the text).

(ii) The vertically integrated price is
\[ p = \frac{c}{1 - 1/\varepsilon} \]
(see chapter 1).


(iii) Firm 2, knowing $p_1$, chooses $p_2$ (or, equivalently, $p = p_1 + p_2$) so as to maximize
\[ (p_2 - c_2)D(p) = |p - (p_1 + c_2)|D(p). \]
Thus, everything is as if firm 2 faced marginal cost $p_1 + c_2$ (recall the analogy with the vertical structure here). From chapter 1, we know that firm 2 chooses
\[ p = \frac{p_1 + c_2}{1 - 1/\varepsilon}. \]
Let $p(p_1)$ denote this final price. ($c_2$ and $\varepsilon$ are constant, so we do not include them in this function.) We have
\[ p' \equiv \frac{dp}{dp_1} = \frac{1}{1 - 1/\varepsilon}. \]
Any increase in the first price is magnified by the second distortion. Firm 1 maximizes
\[ (p_1 - c_1)D(p(p_1)). \]
Hence, by the chain rule of differentiation,
\[ (p_1 - c_1)D'p' + D = 0. \]
Straightforward computations lead to
\[ p = \frac{c}{(1 - 1/\varepsilon)^2}. \]
(iv) In the simultaneous-choice case, firm i chooses $p_i$ so as to maximize
\[ (p_i - c_i)D(p_i + p_j), \]
where $p_j$ is considered as given ($j \neq i$). Thus,
\[ (p_i - c_i)D' + D = 0. \]
Adding these equations for the two firms gives
\[ (p - c)D' + 2D = 0, \]
or
\[ p = \frac{c}{1 - 2/\varepsilon}. \]
Note that $p > c/(1 - 1/\varepsilon)^2$.

Consider the price charged by firm~1 in the simultaneous mode. If in the sequential mode firm~1 charges the same price, then firm~2 will also choose the same price as in the simultaneous mode (since its price is optimal given firm~1's). Now consider a slight decrease in firm~1's price. If firm~2 did not change its price, firm~1's profit would be affected only to the second order (because firm~1's price is optimal given firm~2's). But this also has an effect on firm~2's price, which is reduced (see~(ii)). This effect increases demand, and thus increases firm~1's profit, as $p_1 - c_1 > 0$. Hence, a small price decrease is profitable for firm~1. This type of reasoning will be developed more systematically in chapters~5 and~8.

\[ (p_2 - c_2)D(p) = |p - (p_1 + c_2)|D(p). \]

\[ (p_1 - c_1)D(p(p_1)). \]

\[ (p_1 - c_1)D'p' + D = 0. \]

\[ (p_i - c_i)D' + D = 0. \]
Adding these equations for the two firms gives
\[ (p - c)D' + 2D = 0, \]
or
\[ p = \frac{c}{1 - 2/\varepsilon}. \]

annotator_overrides.question:
Some durable-good producers tie the purchase of spare parts and maintenance to the purchase of the good. (For instance, Boeing used to tie spare parts to the sale of commercial jets through requirements provisions, and required subcontractors to destroy any production overruns of spare parts.) The purpose of this exercise is to show that the input-substitution model offers a possible explanation of this practice. (Can you think of alternative explanations?)

A monopolist produces a durable good at unit cost $c$. A competitive industry offers maintenance at unit cost $c_{\prime}$. Another competitive downstream industry uses the durable good to produce the final output. One unit of the durable good in working condition produces one unit of the final output per unit of time. Time is continuous, and the rate of interest is $r$. The firms in the downstream industry consume $x_{\prime}$ units of maintenance per unit of durable good and per unit of time. The conditional probability of breakdown of the durable good is $\alpha(x_{\prime})dt$ between $t$ and $t + dt$, where $\alpha' < 0$ and $\alpha'' > 0$. (Breakdown means that the durable good must be replaced.) Let $p_w$ denote the price of the durable good (assume that this price is constant over time).

(i) Show that the price of the final good is
\[
 p = c_{\prime} x_{\prime} + p_w [ r + \alpha(x_{\prime})],
\]
where $x_{\prime}$ minimizes the right-hand side.

Compute the competitive price $p$ of the final good as a function of $c_{\prime}$, $x_{\prime}$, $p_w$, $r$, and $\alpha(x_{\prime})$.

Symbols (for final answer):
- `alpha_x_prime`: breakdown hazard rate evaluated at x', i.e., alpha(x')
- `c_prime`: unit cost of maintenance (c')
- `p_w`: price of the durable good
- `r`: interest rate
- `x_prime`: maintenance intensity per unit time (x')

before_annotation.answer:
(Another explanation was given in chapter 3: The tie-in of spare parts or maintenance can be used as a counting device to discriminate between high-use and low-use consumers. For a more general discussion of tie-ins and their link with the input substitution model, see Shughart 1985.)

(i) Informally: The rental cost of the capital (the tie) equals the interest on the durable good, $rp_w$, plus the product of the probability that the good breaks down, $\alpha'(x')$, and the replacement cost, $p_w$. To this must be added the maintenance cost, $c'x'$. Thus, the marginal cost of one unit of output is
\[ \min_{x}|c'x' + p_w|r + \alpha'(x')||. \]
More formally: If the downstream industry buys the durable good, the expected total cost (gross of maintenance costs) of owning one unit of the durable good permanently is
\[ C = p_w + \int_0^\infty \alpha e^{-\alpha t} e^{-rt}Cdt. \]
The owner must pay $p_w$ now. The probability at date 0 of the durable good's breaking down between $t$ and $t + dt$ is $\alpha e^{-\alpha t}$. The owner then faces an expected total cost of $C$ anew. Integrating, we obtain
\[ rC = p_w(r + \alpha). \]
Thus, again, the cost per unit of time is $p_w(r + \alpha) + c'x'$.

(ii) The monopolist sells at $p_w > c$.
The first-order condition for the downstream industry is
\[
c' + p_w \alpha'(x') = 0,
\]
and that for the vertically integrated structure is
\[
c' + c\,\alpha'(x') = 0.
\]

Because $\alpha'' > 0$, $x'$ is smaller under vertical integration. (The result that cost minimization is destroyed by excessive maintenance downstream was obtained by Schmalensee. In contrast with a sale policy, the rental policy yields cost minimization and is preferred by the monopolist.) Sufficient restraints include a tie-in and a royalty.

\medskip
\noindent
(iii) Introduce a fictitious variable,
\[
x \equiv r + \alpha(x').
\]

(Note that $\mathrm{d}x/\mathrm{d}x' < 0$ and $\mathrm{d}^2 x/\mathrm{d}x'^2 > 0$.)
Thus, the downstream industry minimizes
\[
c' x' + p_w x(x').
\]

In the input-substitution problem in the text, the downstream industry minimizes
\[
c' x' + p_w x
\]
subject to $f(x,x') = \text{constant}$ (a cost-minimization program).
The constraint can be written as $x = x(x')$ with
$\mathrm{d}x/\mathrm{d}x' < 0$ (inputs are substitutes) and
$\mathrm{d}^2 x/\mathrm{d}x'^2 > 0$ (the production function is concave).

The purpose of this exercise is to show that the input-substitution model offers a possible explanation of this practice. (Can you think of alternative explanations?)

A monopolist produces a durable good at unit cost $c$. A competitive industry offers maintenance at unit cost $p'_w = c'$. Another competitive downstream industry uses the durable good to produce the final output. One unit of the durable good in working condition produces one unit of the final output per unit of time. Time is continuous, and the rate of interest is $r$. The firms in the downstream industry consume $x'$ units of maintenance per unit of durable good and per unit of time. The conditional probability of breakdown of the durable good is $\alpha(x')dt$ between $t$ and $t + dt$, where $\alpha' < 0$ and $\alpha'' > 0$. (Breakdown means that the durable good must be replaced.) Let $p_w$ denote the price of the durable good (assume that this price is constant over time).

(i) Show that the price of the final good is
\[
p = c' x' + p_w [ r + \alpha(x')],
\]
where $x'$ minimizes the right-hand side.

step5.question: Some durable-good producers tie the purchase of spare parts and maintenance to the purchase of the good. (For instance, Boeing used to tie spare parts to the sale of commercial jets through requirements provisions, and required subcontractors to destroy any production overruns of spare parts.) The purpose of this exercise is to show that the input-substitution model offers a possible explanation of this practice. (Can you think of alternative explanations?) A monopolist produces a durable good at unit cost $c$. A competitive industry offers maintenance at unit cost $c_{\prime}$. Another competitive downstream industry uses the durable good to produce the final output. One unit of the durable good in working condition produces one unit of the final output per unit of time. Time is continuous, and the rate of interest is $r$. The firms in the downstream industry consume $x_{\prime}$ units of maintenance per unit of durable good and per unit of time. The conditional probability of breakdown of the durable good is $\alpha(x_{\prime})dt$ between $t$ and $t + dt$, where $\alpha' < 0$ and $\alpha'' > 0$. (Breakdown means that the durable good must be replaced.) Let $p_w$ denote the price of the durable good (assume that this price is constant over time). (i) Show that the price of the final good is \[ p = c_{\prime} x_{\prime} + p_w [ r + \alpha(x_{\prime})], \] where $x_{\prime}$ minimizes the right-hand side. Compute the competitive price $p$ of the final good as a function of $c_{\prime}$, $x_{\prime}$, $p_w$, $r$, and $\alpha(x_{\prime})$. Symbols (for final answer): - `alpha_x_prime`: breakdown hazard rate evaluated at x', i.e., alpha(x') - `c_prime`: unit cost of maintenance (c') - `p_w`: price of the durable good - `r`: interest rate - `x_prime`: maintenance intensity per unit time (x')
step5.reference_reasoning: In a competitive downstream industry, the final-good price equals the minimum long-run expected cost per unit time of producing one unit of output. Using one durable good yields 1 unit of output per unit time while it works. Maintenance intensity x' costs c' x' per unit time. The durable breaks down with hazard rate alpha(x'), implying expected replacement purchases at rate alpha(x') per unit time, costing alpha(x') p_w per unit time. Holding the durable also ties up capital p_w with opportunity cost r p_w per unit time. Summing these flow costs gives p = c' x' + p_w (r + alpha(x')). The minimizing x' is the one that minimizes this expression.

(Another explanation was given in chapter 3: The tie-in of spare parts or maintenance can be used as a counting device to discriminate between high-use and low-use consumers. For a more general discussion of tie-ins and their link with the input substitution model, see Shughart 1985.)

(i) Informally: The rental cost of the capital (the tie) equals the interest on the durable good, $rp_w$, plus the product of the probability that the good breaks down, $\alpha'(x')$, and the replacement cost, $p_w$. To this must be added the maintenance cost, $c'x'$. Thus, the marginal cost of one unit of output is
\[ \min_{x}|c'x' + p_w|r + \alpha'(x')||. \]
More formally: If the downstream industry buys the durable good, the expected total cost (gross of maintenance costs) of owning one unit of the durable good permanently is
\[ C = p_w + \int_0^\infty \alpha e^{-\alpha t} e^{-rt}Cdt. \]
The owner must pay $p_w$ now. The probability at date 0 of the durable good's breaking down between $t$ and $t + dt$ is $\alpha e^{-\alpha t}$. The owner then faces an expected total cost of $C$ anew. Integrating, we obtain
\[ rC = p_w(r + \alpha). \]
Thus, again, the cost per unit of time is $p_w(r + \alpha) + c'x'$.

(ii) The monopolist sells at $p_w > c$.
The first-order condition for the downstream industry is
\[
c' + p_w \alpha'(x') = 0,
\]
and that for the vertically integrated structure is
\[
c' + c\,\alpha'(x') = 0.
\]

Because $\alpha'' > 0$, $x'$ is smaller under vertical integration. (The result that cost minimization is destroyed by excessive maintenance downstream was obtained by Schmalensee. In contrast with a sale policy, the rental policy yields cost minimization and is preferred by the monopolist.) Sufficient restraints include a tie-in and a royalty.

\medskip
\noindent

\[
x \equiv r + \alpha(x').
\]

before_annotation.answer: (i) A franchise fee is not sufficient. Whatever the marginal wholesale price $p_w$, competition generally does not lead to the vertically integrated level of services. (Actually, $A = 0$ because competition drives retailers' profits down to zero.)
before_annotation.question:
Consider the following model of intrabrand competition and retail services.
The demand function for the final good is
\[
q = D(p,s),
\]
for a package of $p$ (final price) and $s$ (services).
Assume that all consumers are identical.
Their net consumer surplus is $S(p,s)$, where $S$ is defined so that
\[
\frac{\partial S}{\partial p} = -D(p,s).
\]

The retailers, who are all identical, incur a per-unit cost $\Phi(s)$ for service $s$, so that total service cost is $q\,\Phi(s)$.

The vertically integrated profit is obtained by choosing $p^m$ and $s^m$ so as to maximize
\[
[p - c - \Phi(s)]D(p,s).
\]

In the decentralized structure, the manufacturer charges a wholesale price $p_w \ge c$ in order to maximize his profit.
The retailer then maximizes his own profit with respect to $p$ and $s$.
The competitive price and level of services maximize
\[
S(p,s),
\]
subject to
\[
p = p_w + \Phi(s).
\]

To encourage more promotional effort and obtain the vertically integrated profit, the manufacturer can make the retailer a residual claimant by choosing $p_w = c$, and capture the retailer’s residual profit through a franchise fee $A$.

In this model, which of the following instruments is sufficient to obtain the vertically integrated outcome?

(i) a franchise fee

The demand function for the final good is \[ q = D(p,s), \] for a package of $p$ (final price) and $s$ (services). Assume that all consumers are identical. Their net consumer surplus is $S(p,s)$, where $S$ is defined so that \[ \frac{\partial S}{\partial p} = -D(p,s). \] The retailers, who are all identical, incur a per-unit cost $\Phi(s)$ for service $s$, so that total service cost is $q\,\Phi(s)$. The retailer then maximizes his own profit with respect to $p$ and $s$. The competitive price and level of services maximize \[ S(p,s), \] subject to \[ p = p_w + \Phi(s). \] To encourage more promotional effort and obtain the vertically integrated profit, the manufacturer can make the retailer a residual claimant by choosing $p_w = c$, and capture the retailer’s residual profit through a franchise fee $A$. effective_after_annotation.reference_reasoning: A franchise fee by itself does not remove the marginal distortion created by a wholesale price above marginal cost. With only a franchise fee and no control of the per-unit wholesale price, the retailer still chooses (p,s) to maximize consumer surplus subject to p = p_w + Phi(s), so p_w continues to affect the retailer’s marginal incentives and generally prevents replication of the vertically integrated (p^m,s^m). To obtain the integrated outcome, the manufacturer must also set p_w = c (making the retailer a residual claimant) and then use a franchise fee A to extract the resulting profit.

Their net consumer surplus is $S(p,s)$, where $S$ is defined so that \[ \frac{\partial S}{\partial p} = -D(p,s). \] The retailers, who are all identical, incur a per-unit cost $\Phi(s)$ for service $s$, so that total service cost is $q\,\Phi(s)$. The vertically integrated profit is obtained by choosing $p^m$ and $s^m$ so as to maximize \[ [p - c - \Phi(s)]D(p,s). \] In the decentralized structure, the manufacturer charges a wholesale price $p_w \ge c$ in order to maximize his profit. The retailer then maximizes his own profit with respect to $p$ and $s$. The competitive price and level of services maximize \[ S(p,s), \] subject to \[ p = p_w + \Phi(s). \] To encourage more promotional effort and obtain the vertically integrated profit, the manufacturer can make the retailer a residual claimant by choosing $p_w = c$, and capture the retailer’s residual profit through a franchise fee $A$. Judge True/False: In this model, instrument (i) a franchise fee alone is sufficient to obtain the vertically integrated outcome.
question_original:
Consider the following model of intrabrand competition and retail services.
The demand function for the final good is
\[
q = D(p,s),
\]
for a package of $p$ (final price) and $s$ (services).
Assume that all consumers are identical.
Their net consumer surplus is $S(p,s)$, where $S$ is defined so that
\[
\frac{\partial S}{\partial p} = -D(p,s).
\]

The retailers, who are all identical, incur a per-unit cost $\Phi(s)$ for service $s$, so that total service cost is $q\,\Phi(s)$.

The vertically integrated profit is obtained by choosing $p^m$ and $s^m$ so as to maximize
\[
[p - c - \Phi(s)]D(p,s).
\]

In the decentralized structure, the manufacturer charges a wholesale price $p_w \ge c$ in order to maximize his profit.
The retailer then maximizes his own profit with respect to $p$ and $s$.
The competitive price and level of services maximize
\[
S(p,s),
\]
subject to
\[
p = p_w + \Phi(s).
\]

To encourage more promotional effort and obtain the vertically integrated profit, the manufacturer can make the retailer a residual claimant by choosing $p_w = c$, and capture the retailer’s residual profit through a franchise fee $A$.

In this model, which of the following instruments is sufficient to obtain the vertically integrated outcome?

(i) a franchise fee
